lesson.title: HTTP and HTTPS
lesson.objective: Identify common HTTP methods, status codes, and headers
Explain how HTTPS uses TLS to provide security
lesson.definition: An application-layer protocol that defines how clients (browsers) and servers communicate to transfer web content. HTTP is stateless — each request is independent.
lesson.note: HTTP typically uses port 80. HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses port 443 and encrypts all communication using TLS (Transport Layer Security).
lesson.example: 1. You type https://www.example.com in your browser 2. Browser performs DNS lookup to get the server's IP 3. Browser establishes a TCP connection (port 443 for HTTPS) 4. TLS handshake encrypts the connection 5. Browser sends an HTTP GET request 6. Server processes the request and sends an HTTP response 7. Browser renders the HTML response
lesson.codeblock:
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1 Host: www.example.com Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) Connection: keep-alive
HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8 Content-Length: 1256 Cache-Control: max-age=3600
<html>...</html>
lesson.definition: GET: Retrieve a resource (read-only, idempotent) POST: Submit data to create a resource PUT: Replace a resource entirely PATCH: Partially update a resource DELETE: Remove a resource HEAD: Like GET but returns only headers OPTIONS: Query available methods

1xx — Informational (100 Continue) 2xx — Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content) 3xx — Redirection (301 Moved Permanently, 302 Found) 4xx — Client Error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found) 5xx — Server Error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable)
lesson.note: A quick way to remember status codes: 2=happy (success), 4=your fault (client error), 5=our fault (server error).
lesson.definition: HTTPS wraps HTTP in TLS (Transport Layer Security). TLS provides: - Encryption: Data cannot be read by eavesdroppers - Authentication: Server presents a certificate to prove its identity - Integrity: Data cannot be tampered with in transit
lesson.example: 1. Client Hello: Browser sends supported TLS versions and cipher suites 2. Server Hello: Server selects cipher suite, sends its certificate 3. Certificate Verification: Browser verifies the certificate with a trusted CA 4. Key Exchange: Both sides compute a shared secret key 5. Finished: Both sides confirm encryption is active All subsequent HTTP data is encrypted with the negotiated key.
lesson.note: Never send sensitive data (passwords, credit cards) over plain HTTP. Always ensure your site uses HTTPS with a valid certificate. Look for the padlock icon in the browser.

question.text: Which HTTP status code indicates a successful request?
Which port does HTTPS use by default?
Which HTTP method is used to retrieve a web page?
What does TLS stand for and what does it provide?
question.answer: Transport Layer Security — provides encryption, authentication, and data integrity for HTTPS connections

lesson.exercise: Using your browser's Developer Tools (F12 → Network tab): (a) Visit any HTTPS website and inspect the request/response headers (b) Identify the HTTP method, status code, and content-type (c) Click on the security padlock and examine the TLS certificate details (d) Find at least 3 different HTTP response headers and explain their purpose

summary.item: HTTP is a stateless application-layer protocol for transferring web content
HTTP methods: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE (remove)
HTTPS = HTTP + TLS encryption; uses port 443; provides encryption, auth, and integrity
Always use HTTPS for any site handling sensitive data
